actions.label: Hire a Fractional CRO
actions.description: Rent a part-time Chief Revenue Officer who picks and runs your smartest growth move each month.
actions.lesson: You can't afford an A-player full-time yet — so you rent one. A fractional executive is leverage on a budget: real expertise, part-time cost. It buys back your time now and proves out the role before you commit to a full salary.
actions.narrative_success: Your fractional CRO is on. Each month they pick and execute the smartest growth play. Part-time pay scales modestly with the company — when you can afford a full-timer, they'll do far more.
actions.narrative_failure: The fractional CRO is onboarding part-time. They'll be picking your growth moves from here.

actions.label: Promote CRO to Full-Time
actions.description: Make your CRO full-time so they actively build the growth engine — pricey, but powerful at scale.
actions.lesson: This is the ceiling great founders hit: top talent is expensive, and you can only afford it if your capital is working — credit funding growth, a tax-efficient structure protecting cash, and leverage compounding faster than payroll. Brute-force cash can't carry an A-team; efficient leverage can.
actions.narrative_success: Your CRO is full-time now — and it shows. They're building the growth engine every day: capacity, pipeline, brand all climbing on their own. The salary is serious, but at your scale the leverage more than covers it.
actions.narrative_failure: The full-time offer is signed; they're still spinning up the bigger engine. The payroll hit is real from day one.

actions.label: Refine Your Offer
actions.description: Package your expertise into a clear, premium offer so every customer is worth more.
actions.lesson: Sell outcomes, not hours — to people who can pay. A sharper, premium offer raises the value of every single customer you have, which is far more powerful than just adding more of them.
actions.narrative_success: The new pricing feels scary — and every new prospect is saying yes faster than before. You were undercharging.
actions.narrative_failure: Two clients pushed back on the new pricing. You compromised on one. The packaging needs another pass.

actions.label: PR & Brand Campaign
actions.description: Run a brand push — podcasts, press, a refresh — to build authority and charge more.
actions.lesson: Authority is pricing power. When prospects already know your name from a podcast or feature, you're not discounting to win them — they expect to pay a premium, and every customer is worth more.
actions.narrative_success: A local business podcast featured you. Three inbound calls the next week, all mentioning the episode — and none of them flinched at your rates.
actions.narrative_failure: The rebrand looks great. The press pitch didn't land. Brand equity went up; the phone didn't ring more. Yet.

actions.label: Build a Content Engine
actions.description: Hire a content team so your brand grows on autopilot, lifting what every customer is worth.
actions.lesson: A content team makes your brand compound on autopilot. A bigger brand means higher perceived value — you raise prices and capacity without trading more of your hours for attention.
actions.narrative_success: The content calendar runs itself. You approved this month's posts in twenty minutes. Your brand — and your pricing power — keeps climbing.
actions.narrative_failure: The team is producing content. It's... fine. Not your voice yet. Takes time to train a brand voice into other people.

actions.label: License or Franchise
actions.description: Let others run your model under your brand, multiplying the value your name can capture.
actions.lesson: Sell the system, not your time. Licensing your playbook lets others run your model under your brand — multiplying the value your business can capture far beyond what your own delivery could ever reach.
actions.narrative_success: Two licensees signed. They're paying you monthly for the right to use your name and your playbook. This is leverage.
actions.narrative_failure: One licensee signed, then struggled. Supporting them took more time than expected. The model needs refinement.

actions.label: Outbound Prospecting
actions.description: Fill your funnel by hand with cold calls, DMs, and in-person networking to put names in the pipeline.
actions.lesson: Early on, volume wins. Talk to enough of the right people — online and in person — and the math produces leads. In-person especially: people remember someone they've met, and those become your warmest leads. Don't polish the funnel before you've filled it.
actions.narrative_success: Dozens of calls and a few handshakes later, your pipeline has more names in it than it's ever had — and a couple of people approached you for once.
actions.narrative_failure: A lot of calls and business cards, one solid maybe. But that's a lead and a few new faces who now know your name.

actions.label: Social Media Content
actions.description: Post consistently about your work to build an audience that feeds leads over time.
actions.lesson: Organic content compounds slowly but never stops. It won't pay this month — it builds an audience that feeds leads into your funnel for years.
actions.narrative_success: One post went semi-viral in your niche. Your DMs have actual prospects in them for the first time.
actions.narrative_failure: Consistent posting, crickets responding. But the archive is building — it'll compound eventually.

actions.label: Test Paid Ads
actions.description: Run a paid ads test to learn what message lands and turn ad spend into leads fast.
actions.lesson: Master one channel at a time. Paid ads give the fastest feedback and the fastest leads — pour in there first, build organic in parallel for the long game.
actions.narrative_success: The ads are profitable. Not wildly — but the cost per lead is low enough to pour more in. You've found a lever.
actions.narrative_failure: The ad budget mostly evaporated. But you learned what doesn't work, which is worth something.

actions.label: Create a Lead Magnet
actions.description: Offer a free guide or checklist that captures emails — give value first, sell later.
actions.lesson: Give value before you ask for the sale. A free, genuinely useful resource earns trust and an email address — the start of a lead you own.
actions.narrative_success: The lead magnet is live. Fifteen new emails in the first week. These aren't customers yet — but they raised their hand.
actions.narrative_failure: Downloads are trickling in. The topic might need adjusting, but you've got five new leads and a distribution channel.

actions.label: Ask for Referrals
actions.description: Ask every client for warm introductions — the cheapest, highest-trust leads you can get.
actions.lesson: Referrals are the cheapest, highest-trust leads you'll ever get. Happy clients are a sales force you don't pay — but only if you actually ask.
actions.narrative_success: Your best client introduced you to three people at her networking group. Warm leads, already half-sold.
actions.narrative_failure: Everyone said they'd think about it. One person actually came through — better than nothing.

actions.label: Referral & Affiliate Partnerships
actions.description: Set up referral and commission-based affiliate partners whose audiences become your lead channel.
actions.lesson: Borrow other people's trust and audiences. Partners who serve your same client (but don't compete) plus affiliates working on commission turn other people's reach into a steady, near-free lead source — you mostly pay for results.
actions.narrative_success: Partners and affiliates are live — each sending warm leads a month, on near-zero or commission-only cost. Leads from channels you didn't have to build yourself.
actions.narrative_failure: A couple of partners are producing; the rest signed on but haven't sent anyone yet. Follow up and nurture the network next month.

actions.label: Do the Sales Yourself
actions.description: Close deals personally — your cheapest, highest-converting sales channel early on is you.
actions.lesson: Before you can afford a sales team or a funnel, you are the sales team. Founder-led selling is the cheapest, highest-converting channel you have early on — and every call teaches you what actually makes people buy.
actions.narrative_success: You closed a handful of deals by sheer hustle — calls, follow-ups, and asking for the sale. It's exhausting, but the pipeline turned into paying customers.
actions.narrative_failure: A lot of calls, a few maybes, one yes. Selling is a numbers game and this month the numbers were thin — but you learned what objections to expect.

actions.label: Offer a Discount to Drive Sales
actions.description: Slash prices for a flood of customers — but they're demanding bargain-hunters who erode your margins.
actions.lesson: Discounting is the easiest way to get customers and the hardest way to build a business. Cheap clients are the most demanding and the least loyal — you trade margin, pricing power, and energy for volume that doesn't compound. Use it sparingly, if at all.
actions.narrative_success: The promo worked — a flood of new customers signed up. But they haggle over every invoice, demand round-the-clock attention, and your margins (and your evenings) took the hit.
actions.narrative_failure: The discount pulled in a handful of price-shoppers and cheapened how people see you. A lot of effort for thin, fickle business.

actions.label: Launch a Webinar Funnel
actions.description: Build an automated webinar that pitches and converts leads 24/7 while you sleep.
actions.lesson: Build it once, it sells forever. An automated funnel turns your best pitch into an asset that converts leads 24/7 — lifting your close rate without costing you time.
actions.narrative_success: The webinar converted at 8%. People are signing up, watching, and buying — at 2 AM while you're asleep.
actions.narrative_failure: Registration was decent. Conversion was terrible. The offer needs reworking, but the funnel infrastructure is built.

actions.label: Hire a Salesperson
actions.description: Hire a dedicated closer to lift conversion so you stop being the sales bottleneck.
actions.lesson: A dedicated closer lifts conversion on the leads you already have — more customers from the same pipeline. But sales talent is paid on results: their cost rises as revenue rises, so the real leverage comes from a better product and a warm pipeline, not just adding salespeople.
actions.narrative_success: Your new closer is converting leads you'd have let go cold. Conversion is up — and you spent the month on the business instead of in it.
actions.narrative_failure: The new hire is ramping slowly. Conversion hasn't moved much yet, but you got ten hours a week back.

actions.label: Install CRM & Pipeline
actions.description: Build a real sales pipeline with stages and follow-ups so fewer leads slip away.
actions.lesson: What gets tracked gets closed. A real pipeline surfaces the leads you'd otherwise forget and lifts your conversion — most lost sales die from neglect, not rejection.
actions.narrative_success: For the first time, you can see every prospect, where they are, and what happens next. Three forgotten leads turned into deals.
actions.narrative_failure: The CRM is set up but half your data is still in sticky notes. Partial improvement — you'll migrate the rest next month.

actions.label: Launch Email Nurture Campaign
actions.description: Build an email list and send weekly value emails that warm leads until they convert.
actions.lesson: An email list is an asset YOU own — not rented from an algorithm. Nurture it and cold leads warm into buyers, lifting conversion on the pipeline you already paid to fill.
actions.narrative_success: The email sequence is converting. Three leads became clients this month — they said your emails made them feel like they already knew you.
actions.narrative_failure: Open rates are decent, clicks are low. The content needs work, but the infrastructure is built.
